talk_metadata.talk_title: BIGHAT-299: First-in-class CCAM6-directed TCE for diverse solid malignancies
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "the problem that's derailed a number of prior attempts to drug this target is expression in the myeloid tree"
Speaker said: "we are very proudly proceeding quickly towards the clinic. IND is expected in the second half of this year"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Prior CCAM6-directed ADCs and TCEs (Genentech DM1 ADC, Bayer tinirelimab)
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: High-affinity binding to CCAM6 on mature neutrophils causing peripheral antigen sink and dose‑limiting cytopenias
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted substantial on‑target cytopenias that are dose‑limiting because of an overwhelming peripheral antigen sink

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: CCAM6‑directed T‑cell engager with conditional activity based on antigen density
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolling all patients with CCAM6‑positive solid tumors without stratifying by neutrophil CCAM6 expression levels
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Quantitative peripheral neutrophil CCAM6 density to predict on‑target toxicity

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that the CCAM6 binder is a fully human VHH with weak affinity (3 µM KD) and relies on engineered geometry; the core scaffold is not protected by novel IP beyond the VHH sequence
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad indication enrollment may dilute signal given heterogeneous CCAM6 expression across tumor types

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that BIGHAT‑299 has a 1,000‑fold differential IC50 between cancer cells and neutrophils, then concluded that the asset offers a "credible first‑in‑class opportunity" despite acknowledging peripheral antigen sink issues in prior programs

key_data_gaps: No human Phase 1 safety or PK data presented

crispro_opportunity.description: Need for quantitative peripheral neutrophil CCAM6 expression to predict toxicity and select patients
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted substantial on‑target cytopenias due to peripheral antigen sink on mature neutrophils
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO could model CCAM6 density distributions and predict safe dosing windows

crispro_opportunity.description: Potential escape via down‑regulation of CCAM6 on tumor cells
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker set a breakpoint at 80,000 copies per cell based on biopsy data
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate tumor heterogeneity and identify combination strategies to prevent antigen loss

cited_competitors.name: Genentech
cited_competitors.context: Developed a DM1 CCAM6 ADC that failed due to peripheral neutrophil toxicity

cited_competitors.name: Bayer
cited_competitors.context: Advanced tinirelimab (CCAM6 TCE) that failed with similar toxicity

unresolved_questions: What is the minimal CCAM6 density on tumor cells required for durable clinical responses?
Will CCAM6 expression on normal epithelial tissues cause off‑target effects at therapeutic doses?

talk_metadata.talk_title: ENA‑101: First‑in‑class bispecific T‑cell engager targeting DART‑fox peptide‑HLA
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We believe this is a really promising target, has all the hallmarks of a target that you'd want to pursue with sensitive T cell engager type molecules"
Speaker said: "We have identified no risk peptides to date with that approach"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Dependence on HLA‑A3 prevalence (~20‑30% of population) limits patient eligibility
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted that "10%‑20% of all new cancer diagnoses" could be addressed, implying a subset response

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: ENA‑101 IND‑enabling preclinical program (no NCT disclosed)
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Bispecific T‑cell engager against DART‑fox peptide presented by HLA‑A3
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No pre‑screening for HLA‑A3 status in early studies could dilute efficacy signal

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed detailed affinity maturation and binding data, suggesting the core scaffold is conventional IgG with engineered CDRs
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on a single HLA allele limits market size and may require extensive HLA typing

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated sub‑10 pM killing potency in vitro yet emphasized that "the evidence of the DART‑fox expression in major solid tumors gives us routes towards recruiting all comers" despite HLA‑A3 restriction

key_data_gaps: No in vivo efficacy data presented
No safety data in animal models

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a CrisPRO model to predict peptide‑HLA presentation levels across tumor types and HLA‑A3 carriers
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted HLA‑A3 prevalence of 20‑30% and variable peptide density (hundreds to low thousands copies per cell)
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate proteomic and HLA‑binding predictions to prioritize patients

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify synergistic partners (e.g., checkpoint inhibitors) to broaden efficacy beyond HLA‑A3 restricted cohort
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted synergy with existing immunotherapies in discussion
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate combination effects on T‑cell activation thresholds

cited_competitors.name: Atezolizumab
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as a checkpoint inhibitor that could be combined with ENA‑101

unresolved_questions: Will ENA‑101 trigger off‑target T‑cell activation against other peptide‑HLA complexes?
What is the durability of responses in HLA‑A3 positive patients?

target_biology: Peptide‑linked NRP1 antibody delivering viral epitopes for TCR‑engineered T‑cell killing
flawed_enrollment_criteria: Model uses NRP1‑high tumor lines; does not account for heterogeneous or low NRP1 expression in patients
missing_biomarker: Quantitative tumor NRP1 density threshold for efficacy

ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed the binding domain (B1B2) and linker chemistry, indicating a conventional antibody scaffold with a cleavable linker
clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on pre‑existing viral memory T‑cells; patient HLA‑A2 restriction limits applicability

key_data_gaps: No in vivo efficacy data in immunocompetent models
No assessment of HLA‑A2 prevalence in target population

description: Model heterogeneity in NRP1 expression and protease activity to predict which tumors will present sufficient peptide
transcript_evidence: Speaker noted variability in NRP1 levels and asked about low‑NRP1 tumors
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate protease‑driven linker cleavage and peptide loading across tumor sub‑clones

description: Incorporate HLA‑A2 typing and NRP1 expression thresholds into future trial eligibility
transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that normal NRP1‑expressing cells were not killed, suggesting a need for biomarker selection
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model patient‑level biomarker distributions to optimize inclusion criteria

talk_title: Tumor‑activated PrimeBody platform enables more potent CD47 targeting
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We see no anemia" despite using a high‑affinity CD47 binder
Speaker said: "Our linkers show at least three‑fold increased activity over clinical benchmarks across all indications"

mechanistic_blindspot: Ubiquitous CD47 expression creates a massive tissue sink and on‑target anemia
hidden_tox_signal: Speaker highlighted anemia as the primary dose‑limiting toxicity of existing CD47 programs

target_biology: High‑affinity SIRPα CD47 binder with masked activation in tumor microenvironment
flawed_enrollment_criteria: Preclinical models do not stratify tumors by protease activity that drives linker cleavage
missing_biomarker: Tumor protease expression profile to ensure selective unmasking

ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed detailed affinity‑based mask and protease‑cleavable linker designs, suggesting a conventional protein engineering approach
clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on tumor‑specific protease activity may limit applicability across low‑protease tumors

key_data_gaps: No human safety or PK data for VORA101
Unclear durability of masking in chronic dosing

description: Model tumor protease expression to select indications where linker cleavage is maximized
transcript_evidence: Speaker selected indications based on protease dysregulation and macrophage presence
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate protease expression datasets to rank indication suitability

description: Apply PrimeBody masking to other high‑affinity CD47 antibodies that failed due to anemia
transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that legacy CD47 programs suffer from tissue sink and anemia
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict masking efficiency for existing CD47 binders

name: Migrolimab
context: Head‑to‑head preclinical comparison showing anemia with migrolimab but not with PrimeBody

name: Avaracept (ALX‑148)
context: Referenced as a high‑affinity SIRPα fusion with a null Fc that required combination with trastuzumab

talk_title: Integrated CD2 costimulation on CD3 T‑cell engagers (Evolve‑104)
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "CD2 is highly expressed on >80% of tumor‑infiltrating T cells"
Speaker said: "Affinity‑tuned Evolve outperformed conventional bispecifics in vitro and in vivo"

mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of co‑stimulatory signal (signal 2) leading to suboptimal T‑cell activation and exhaustion

target_biology: Trispecific T‑cell engager with CD3, tumor antigen (ULBP256), and CD2 co‑stimulation
flawed_enrollment_criteria: Potential inclusion of patients with low CD58 (CD2 ligand) expression could dilute signal

ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker described the CD2 agonist as a conventional antibody fragment; core scaffold may be less protected by novel IP
clinical_strategy_weakness: Potential CRS amplification due to added CD2 activation

key_data_gaps: Long‑term safety and CRS profile not yet reported
Efficacy in HLA‑diverse patient populations not shown

description: Model synergistic effects of CD2 co‑stimulation with checkpoint inhibitors to mitigate CRS
transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that CD2 is highly expressed across TILs and improves activation
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate cytokine release dynamics with varying CD2 affinity

description: Identify tumors with high CD58 expression for optimal response to Evolve‑104
transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted CD2/CD58 as the key co‑stimulatory axis
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate tumor RNA‑seq data to predict CD58 levels

name: Conventional CD3‑only bispecifics
context: Compared head‑to‑head with Evolve platform in vitro and in vivo

talk_title: Preclinical evaluation and safety of CVX250, a bispecific T‑cell engager targeting Cathepsin G peptide‑HLA in AML
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We observed robust killing even at 33 copies per cell"
Speaker said: "We saw no meaningful cytokine induction compared to positive controls"

mechanistic_blindspot: Dependence on HLA‑A0201 (~40% of US population) limits patient eligibility
ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted activity down to 33 peptide copies per cell, implying a subset response

trial_name_or_nct: CVX250 Phase 1 AML trial (no NCT disclosed)
target_biology: Bispecific T‑cell engager targeting CG1 peptide presented by HLA‑A0201 on AML cells
flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolling all HLA‑A0201 positive AML patients without pre‑screening for CG1 peptide density
missing_biomarker: Quantitative CG1/HLA copy number on patient blasts

ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that the Fc contains a silencing LALA‑KA mutation but still shows weak FcγR2A binding at high concentrations, indicating conventional IgG scaffold
clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on a single peptide‑HLA restricts market size

key_data_gaps: No in vivo efficacy data in AML mouse models presented
No assessment of off‑target activity in FcγR2A‑expressing cells beyond platelets

description: Develop a CrisPRO model to predict CG1 peptide presentation levels across AML sub‑types
transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted variability from 15 to ~8,000 predicted copies per cell
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate proteomics and HLA‑binding predictions to prioritize patients

description: Apply masking or affinity tuning to improve safety of other peptide‑HLA bispecifics that showed FcγR2A binding
transcript_evidence: Speaker noted weak FcγR2A interaction at high concentrations
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate Fc engineering impacts on off‑target binding

name: Tebentafusp
context: Mentioned as an example of a peptide‑HLA bispecific with uniform antigen expression

talk_title: First‑in‑class monoclonal antibody targeting TM4SF5 in gastrointestinal cancers
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "TM4SF5 is highly expressed in GI cancers and drives an immunosuppressive TME"
Speaker said: "RCT1213 achieved ~80% tumor growth inhibition, comparable to atezolizumab/bevacizumab"

mechanistic_blindspot: Difficulty in generating antibodies that recognize native conformation of a tetraspanin; potential off‑target binding to other tetraspanins

target_biology: Monoclonal antibody against extracellular loops of TM4SF5
flawed_enrollment_criteria: Potential inclusion of patients without confirmed TM4SF5 overexpression could dilute efficacy
missing_biomarker: Quantitative TM4SF5 expression threshold for patient selection

ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker described using a virus‑like lipoparticle for immunization, suggesting a conventional IgG scaffold; core epitope is conformational and may be difficult to protect by patents
clinical_strategy_weakness: Target is a tetraspanin with potential expression in normal tissues; safety margin not yet demonstrated

key_data_gaps: No safety data in non‑human primates
No clinical data on TM4SF5 expression heterogeneity in patients

description: Model TM4SF5 expression across GI tumor cohorts to define a cutoff for patient enrollment
transcript_evidence: Speaker presented survival differences between high and low TM4SF5 expression groups
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate TCGA RNA‑seq and proteomics to predict high‑expressor subpopulations

description: Predict synergistic benefit of RCT1213 with checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., atezolizumab) across TM4SF5‑high tumors
transcript_evidence: Speaker reported triple combination achieving complete responses in preclinical models
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate immune‑modulatory effects of TM4SF5 blockade plus ICI

name: Atezolizumab
context: Used as a comparator in HCC xenograft model

name: Bevacizumab
context: Combined with atezolizumab in HCC model
